title: Postnatal ventral midbrain dopamine neuronal culture protocols
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"fc5b8","text":"This protocol details the specific steps and media solutions required to dissect and triturate the postnatal netral midbrain dopamine neuronal cultures on mice.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"dno53","text":"Acknowledgments ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8rkna","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"anbln","text":"Thanks to Steve Rayport, who initiated this project, and Serge Przedborski for TH immunolabel and cryosectioning of the figure in the dissection section, and to Robert Burke for collaboration on growth factor studies in this system. Thanks also to the technicians in our lab who advanced these methods, particularly Viviana Davila, Johanna Bogulavsky, Gerald Behr, Anthonia Hananiya, Kester Phillips, Candace Makeda Moore, Carl St. Remy, Megan Antonelli, from our lab, and Geetha Rajendran of Steve Rayport’s lab. Thanks to Christian Bjerggaard, who visited our lab to learn these techniques in April 2004, and revised and added to an earlier addition of this booklet. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bjc6m","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4sosg","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2kdtg","text":"Introduction ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"d1q5c","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"48v4p","text":"This manual is designed to instruct in a detailed manner the steps our group uses to produce postnatally-derived ventral midbrain cultures. The approach used here has been used in numerous studies from our and Stephen Rayport’s labs (Sulzer and Rayport, 1990; Rayport et al., 1992; Sulzer et al., 1993; Cubells et al., 1994; Rayport and Sulzer, 1995; Pothos et al., 1996; Przedborski et al., 1996; Sulzer et al., 1996; Fon et al., 1997; Mena et al., 1997a; Mena et al., 1997b; Burke et al., 1998; Pothos et al., 1998; Sulzer et al., 1998; Kholodilov et al., 1999; Pothos et al., 2000; Sulzer et al., 2000). In addition, these cultures have been used in studies from Louis Trudeau’s lab (Bourque and Trudeau, 2000; Michel and Trudeau, 2000; Congar et al., 2002), Susan Amara’s lab (Prasad and Amara, 2001; Ingram et al., 2002) and Mark Cookson’s groups (Petrucelli et al., 2002). ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"49o5a","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5gk89","text":"Previously, dissociated culture studies of dopamine neurons use embryonically-derived cultures that produce a fraction of dopamine neurons of 1% or less. The postnatally derived cultures developed by Stephen Rayport and myself when I was a postdoc in his lab, have several advantages, the most striking being that the fraction of dopamine neurons is between 20-70%. The fraction is chiefly depending upon the area dissected. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4h8ee","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2eras","text":"Other advantages are that the neurons are more mature than embryonically-derived neurons. Not only do many more display tyrosine hydroxylase (TH), but the dopamine uptake transporter appears to be more highly expressed in the postnatally-derived neurons. Under optimal conditions, these cultures have survived for as long as 6 months, although most survive about 2 months. We suspect that the death is mostly due to increased osmolarity of the medium, and could probably be controlled by weekly partial exchange with conditioned media, testing the osmolarity of the medium over time with an osmometer and compensating with the addition of water, using a dish lid that allows air transfer but not evaporation. The latter approach has recently been introduced by Steve Potter, but has not been attempted to date for the postnatal dopamine neurons. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4o81k","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"etruu","text":"The techniques shown here have also been adapted by our group to produce postnatal cultures of other brain areas, such as the dorsal striatum. However, we suspect that the dopamine neurons are particularly challenging to culture, mostly because dopamine easily oxidizes to produce fairly potent neurotoxins. An odd choice for a neurotransmitter; perhaps we will eventually discover why the property of auto-oxidation to a reactive substance provides a selective advantage. Alternatively, maybe catecholamines were chiefly useful as skin and fur pigment, and simply adapted to produce additional neurotransmitters!","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5bs71","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"vtam","text":"Overview of Techniques","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":22}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"frfg2","text":"First, we outline how we prepare the glass coverslip petri dishes in our lab. While we have used various approaches, including multiwell plates, to good effect, we generally culture on glass coverslips that are glued to petri dishes, so that the neurons produce small, dense cultures. This appears to keep them healthier, and to require no feeding with new media. The glass allows optimal microscopy with DIC/Nomarski imaging and fluorescent imaging. The central glass well allows all of the neurons to be approached with electrodes. We also make neuronal cultures on free-floating coverslips, so they can be removed from the dish for microscopy later. An alternate approach we also outline is the use of Aclar coverslips, which are well adapted for processing for electron microscopy. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8pqhf","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"98pc4","text":"We have found it necessary to culture these neurons on glia monolayers. We have also attempted the “Banker” technique, in which glia are grown close by on an inverted coverslip. However, to date, the dopamine neurons die within 3 days unless they actually touch the glia. Thus, we outline the techniques to produce glia monolayers followed by a protocol how to dissociate the neurons and plate them onto the glia. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cpgq0","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"796n9","text":"We use similar techniques for culturing of other types of neurons (hippocampal, cortical, striatal, etc.). ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6pki9","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"187fp","text":"Figure indicating dissection of an TH immunolabel in mouse sections","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":67}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8qqhh","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":{}},{"key":"2v553","text":"Special Instructions for Mouse Neurons","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":38}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"663l0","text":"Some types of mouse tails (for ex, VMATs) need to be saved for genotyping. Prepare a numbered 1.5ml tube for each mouse. After the mouse has been decapitated, remove the tail using a fresh, sterile razor blade and place tail in numbered tube. Put all tubes on ice during the dissection. After dissection, store tubes in -80°C freezer. Alternatively, number the pups with the marker on their back, cut the tails and genotype the pups before the cultures, and no later than 1- day old. This way the pups with the same genotype can be cultured together rather than individually.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"image","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"61FED64F341F11EE9D4302C0B41BC903","height":552,"is_video":false,"legend":"{\"blocks\":null,\"entityMap\":null}","mime":"image/png","original_name":"image.png","placeholder":"https://content.protocols.io/public/df848d4f6b61dfb9aa0bfd12ff51646a1be6af62bf0dabe832af6448db31296c/cpwmbcasf.png","source":"https://content.protocols.io/files/dkvubcasf.jpg","type":1,"webp_source":"https://content.protocols.io/files/dkvtbcasf.webp","width":550}}}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"67i7o","text":"Three Days Before Culturing","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":27}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"499oc","text":" 1. Make sure that there is an ample supply of glass dishes, Sylgard circles, Techtips and both types of plastic pipette tips (for 1000uL and 200uL). ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5na7i","text":" 2. Figure out the number of dishes and types of neurons required. Calculate the number of pups needed. Pups needed vary by dissection technique, but we have found 1 rat cortex is enough material to easily plate ~110-130 dished, and one mouse VM enough to plate 2-3 dishes.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"92fm","text":" 3. Clean and sterilize dissection tools (This should be done right after each dissection!) ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"a3vhr","text":"        a. First wipe clean with 70% ethanol. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6jh3q","text":"        b. Wipe again with 100% ethanol to speed drying process. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9j68d","text":"        c. Let dry! ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"c4cqd","text":"        d. Leave in the oven at 200°C for at least 1 hour. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4qecq","text":" 4. Clean and sterilize the plastic rings and magnetic stir-bars: ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"80omq","text":"        a. Wipe off any debris from rings and stir-bars. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"33k3p","text":"        b. Let soak in 1N HCL for 1 hour. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"a84vf","text":"        c. Rinse at least 20 times with de-ionized water and let dry. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"65qip","text":"        d. Place rings into 100 ml beakers and individually distribute the stir-bars into small tubes (the 1.5 ml snap-top                 tubes are fine) and autoclave.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"au4lg","text":" 5. Once-a-month, autoclave the flask and glass tube (NOT THE RUBBER STOPPER) that is used for bubbling the carbogen through water for papain dissociation. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"97kbu","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4a3ci","text":"On the Day Before Culturing","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":27}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cpbbf","text":"Prepare appropriate media as needed (SF1C, M10C-G, PBS).","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"abh74","text":"For neuronal culturing, take glial dishes and wash them twice with 2ml of cold MEM. Replace MEM with 2 ml of SF1C. Alternatively, neurobasal medium A/B27 can also be used (Table 16.9). While A/B27 is easier to make, we have had slightly better success with SF1C in certain toxicity assays (unpublished observations). ","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":22}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"en0jt","text":"Place sterile ring in each dish to the side of the glass coverslip well, using forceps with sterile tips. (see dish and media prep. for neurons). For free-floating coverslips, place the ring in the middle of a coverslip.","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"71eeb","text":"Leave dishes with SF1C in the incubator overnight. This allows the media to condition over the glial cells. Alternatively, use preconditioned SF1C media on the day of the culture.","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"acvnp","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"27a38","text":"TO CONDITION MEDIA: ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":19}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1uduc","text":"- have T-225 flask with astrocytes monolayer prepared at least 10 days prior; ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3tfvc","text":"- replace media in the flask with freshly made 200ml of M10CG media. Incubate for 1-2 days. Collect conditioned media and filter to sterilize.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7aevk","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8ilas","text":"To condition neuronal media, add 200ml of SF1C media to the flask with astrocytes and incubate overnight to 2 days. Collect conditioned media and filter-sterilize.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Cut Aclar sheets into squares ~ 1.5 cm and drop into Nanopure dd H 2 O for 00:05:00 .
Swirl the squares in a beaker (if they don’t all immediately float to the top).
Rinse twice in Nanopure dd H 2 O for 5 min:
Expose to 70% ethanol in Nanopure dd H 2 O for 00:30:00 .
Rinse twice in Nanopure dd H 2 O for 5 min:
Expose to 1 Molarity (M), HCl in Nanopure dd H 2 O for 00:30:00 .
Rinse again several times in water.
Maintain in 70% ethanol, covered.
Sylgard the squares to the dishes after the holes are prepared:

Wearing gloves, prepare about 5 mL, Sylgard in a large weighing boat by combining 10 parts resin with 1 part catalyst (by weight) and stirring extensively with flame-sealed Pasteur pipette. Pour contents into new weighing boat to avoid use of resin without catalyst.
Dip an empty 15 ml Falcon tube or glass scintillation vial upside-down into the Sylgard, using it as a rubber stamp to make a circle of Sylgard around the hole, on the outside of the dish.
Place the Aclar sheets on the circle of Sylgard, covering the hole.
Allow Sylgard to spread (about 00:15:00 ).
To cure the Sylgard, place dishes, upside-down, at 37 °C Overnight , or at room temperature for 2 to 3 days (cured Sylgard is no longer sticky to the touch).
Store dishes in zip lock bags: initial and date.
Coat with PDL:
Add 200 µL, 40 µg/ml PDL solution of the to each dish. Expose for 02:00:00 - 04:00:00 at Room temperature in a culture hood without UV light.
Rinse 3 times for 5 min each in tissue culture water: Rinse 1/3: rinse in tissue culture water for 00:05:00 .

Remove water and expose to UV for 01:00:00 to sterilize. Note that these dishes must be coated with laminin before use, just as we do with glass coverslips (see glia dissection protocol) Left over sylgard from dish preparation can be used to make sylgard circles.
Drop 100 coverslips (15 x 15 mm) one-at-a-time into a 200 ml beaker containing 100 mL, 95% EtOH . (Leave approx. 00:01:00 at least)

Prepare a solution of poly-d-lysine in “nanopure” or tissue culture grade water. The final concentration should be 40 microgram per milliliter (μg/mL) and 250mL are adequate for 100 coverslips. Place the poly-Dlysine solution in a glass dish of enough area to give 3/4”/2 cm height to the poly-D lysine solution. (the walls of the dish should be at least 1.5”/4 cm, the area no less than 40”/100 cm square)
Throw (pour) away excess EtOH. Place ethanol soaked cover slips on to a paper or glass container.
Individually flame dry the coverslips (one pass through flame); use forceps.
After putting the slip through the flame, allow it to cool in the air in forceps for approximately 00:00:10 while moving back and forth (cracking or sizzling sounds mean it did not cool enough).
Put the coverslip into the poly-D-lysine solution. Repeat for all slips.
Let sit for 01:00:00 , then pour off the poly-D-lysine, then let slips dry ( Overnight in the hood with the blower on).
Prepare dishes with 10 mm holes punched with a bench punch for wells where neurons and glia will grow. Sylgard the coverslips to the dishes after the holes are prepared.

Wearing gloves, prepare about 5 mL, Sylgard in a large weighing boat by combining 10 parts resin with 1 part catalyst (by weight) and stirring extensively with flame-sealed Pasteur pipette. Pour contents into new weighing boat to avoid use of resin without catalyst.

Dip an empty 15 ml Falcon tube or glass scintillation vial upside-down into the Sylgard, using it as a rubber stamp to make a circle of Sylgard around the hole. Use caution when applying the Sylgard as too much will result in excess Sylgard covering the culturing surface of the coverslip whereas inadequate Sylgard will result in a leaky well.
Place the coverslips on the circle of Sylgard, covering the hole.
Press down on coverslip gently using forceps to eliminate bubbles.
Allow Sylgard to spread (about 00:15:00 ).

To cure the Sylgard, place dishes, upside-down, at 37 °C Overnight , or at room temperature for 2 to 3 days (cured Sylgard is no longer sticky to the touch). Once the dishes are cured they can be stored in zip-lock bags at 4 °C until needed. Note that these glass coverslips must be coated with laminin before use (see glia dissection protocol Left over sylgard from dish preparation can be used to make sylgard circles. If these coverslips ever become unavailable, the Bellco #2 photoetched coverslips have been recommended
These are used for triturating cells Obtain a box of 1000 ul pipette tips (unfiltered). Wear gloves.
Flame all tips in order to seal off hole (we recently started using tips without sealing the hole, making additional holes with needles as described below).

Using a needle, poke a hole as close to the tip as possible through one side and out the other. This will result in two holes. The purpose of piercing right through both sides is to prevent cells getting caught in the pipette. Rather, they will have an outlet hole. Make 3 types of tips, with Large (21G 1½), Medium (22G 1½) and Small (26G 1½ ) size holes.
Autoclave as usual.
For use during dissection as a platform to do the cutting: Weigh out the Elastomer Base and the Elastomer Curing Agent. Mix in the ratio 10:1
Mix well and pour into 12 micro well plate, about 3-4 mm in height.
Cure in oven at 60 °C for 00:30:00 or at Room temperature for 5-7 days.
Cut circles out of wells using razor and individually wrap each circle in aluminum foil, autoclave to sterilize. Left over sylgard from dish preparation can be used to make sylgard circles.
LAMININ COATING Before the dissection/digestion time, dilute 1 aliquot of laminin (140ul of 0.5mg/ml laminin = 70 µg) in 7 mL, sterile MEM . Final concentration 10 microgram per milliliter (μg/mL) .
Prepare the papain dissociation solution. Each vial should hold tissue from a maximum of 2 animals though it’s better to digest 1 rat cortex per vial.

Sterilize the top of each Nunc specimen vial (25 ml) with ethanol. Punch two holes in the top with an 18 gauge needle, using a separate needle to bore holes. In one hole place a new needle with a 0.22 µm filter attached. Leave the other hole empty for ventilation.

Add an HCl-cleaned, autoclaved micro-stir bar to the vial. Fill each tube with 5 mL - 10 mL, freshly made papain solution (sterile filtered with 0.22 µm filter). Place in temperature controlled 37 °C water bath with magnetic stirrers.
Arrange dissection tools, microscope, dissection light, a bunch of transfer pipettes, sterile Petri dish with Sylgard circle, beaker, and 70% ethanol in a 50 ml tube in the sterile hood.
Prepare two buckets of ice to chill the PBS and for the pups.
Cut aluminum foil squares (about 15 x 15 cm).
Perfuse dissociation tube (papain tube) with a steady flow of humidified carbogen (95% oxygen, 5% CO2) delivered through the filter/needle (flow rate adjusted to about 1 bubble per second).

When the papain has reached a pH of pH 7.2 - pH 7.4 in about 00:30:00 - 01:00:00 (indicated by a red color; purple indicates that the papain solution is too alkaline and orange that it is too acidic), anesthetize pups and begin dissection.
Anesthetize pups with an intraperitoneal injection of ketamine (0.01 and 0.05 ml of 100 mg/ml for mouse and rat pups, respectively).
When pups begin to show sedation, put them on ice till hypothermic.
Rinse aluminum foil square, scissors and head with 70% EtOH.
Decapitate pup, allowing head to fall onto aluminum foil square and move to hood.

Remove the olfactory lobes and brain stem (with V-cut). Hemi-sect brain and then peel off meninges. Shell out cortex, taking care to remove the hippocampus. Remove any torn areas to avoid DNA release. Cut into 1 mm 3 chunks.

DIGESTION Place chunks in papain as with neuronal prep, without kynurenate (1 cortex/vial). Chunks will be digested enough to triturate during the initial 00:05:00 - 00:10:00 . We judge by the appearance of the chunks (like cotton candy) when they are ready.
DISSECTION Obtain one or two P1-P3 rats.

Add at least 100 µL, 10 µg/mL laminin per round well (enough to cover the surface) for minimum 01:00:00 at Room temperature (can be done O/N at +4°C) before plating cells and leave in flow bench. Aspirate off laminin and wash each well with MEM. Place into 37 °C incubator to warm dishes before plating the cells. 1 aliquot is enough for about 50-60 dishes.
TRITURATION AND PLATING Warm up 45 mL, sterile M10C-G for rinsing and trituration.
From this point forward, everything should be done in a sterile laminar hood with sterile technique.
Remove chunks from enzyme vial using transfer pipette and place in 15 ml conical test tube.
Rinse chunks 3 times using 2 mL, M10C-G , allowing them to settle each time and discard supernatant:
Using 1 mL, warm M10CG triturate up to a maximum of 10 times (3-5 times should be sufficient) using a regular 1 ml pipette tip.
Remove supernatant to a separate tube, and replace the volume with 1 mL, fresh M10C-G .
Repeat trituration using the tech tips with smaller holes each time until the chunks have all fallen apart.
Pellet the glial cells at 1000 rpm, °C, 5 minutes .
. Resuspend pellet in 10 mL, M10C-G , and count the cells using hemocytometer
Dilute to plating density of 1,000,000-1,500,000 cells/ml.
Remove laminin from wells and plate about 80,000 cells per well. Plate by dripping the cell suspension from about 10 cm above the dish to help minimize number of surviving neurons in glial prep.
Tap the sides of the trays (not for island cultures) in order to evenly distribute cell suspension.
Allow cells to settle for 01:30:00 - 02:00:00 in incubator. For island preps allow 2-5 hours.
Chill M10C-G in meantime.
RINSING AND FEEDING For monolayers: squirt 2 mL, cold MEM into each well, moving the pipette around in a circular motion to wash each well evenly.
Aspirate off MEM and repeat wash:

For island cultures: One dish at a time, lift the cover and tilt the dish at a 45° angle with the hood countertop. Using a transfer pipette very gently, drip 2 mL, cold MEM onto a spot on the dish just above the well so that it slides down the incline and over the well.
Aspirate off the MEM.
Feed with 2 mL, cold M10C-G .
Add FDU to the dishes when cells are ~70% confluent (about 3-5 days). If neurons are still present at this point, remove the medium and cold-feed with 2 mL, M10C-G before adding FDU.
Astrocytes dishes should be at least one week old and at most 4 weeks old at the time the neurons are plated. Feed with cold M10C-G at one month if not already used.
Please see "Before Starting" in Guidelines & Warnings section on Things to Prepare during the Week before Plating Glia and Neurons.
First, turn on the dissociation water bath and let warm to 37 °C .
Make the papain dissociation solution (see Media section). Each vial can hold tissue from 1 to maximum 10 mice preps.

Sterilize the top of each Nunc specimen vial with EtOH. Make two holes in the top with an 18 gauge needle, using a separate needle to bore holes. In one hole place a new needle with a 0.22 µm filter attached. Leave the other hole empty for ventilation.
Arrange dissection tools, microscope, dissection light, a bunch of transfer pipettes, sterile Petri dish with sylgard circle, beaker, and 70% ethanol in a 50 ml tube in the sterile hood.
Get 2 buckets of ice to chill the PBS and to anesthetize the pups.
Rip aluminum foil squares (about 15 cm 2 ).
Fill “insulin” syringe with 100 mg/ml ketamine solution.
Obtain as many P1-P3 rat or mouse pups as needed (animals which are one to three days old)
Superfuse dissociation tube (papain tube) with a steady flow of humidified carbogen delivered through the filter/needle (Flow rate adjusted to about 1 bubble per second).
When the papain has reached a pH of pH 7.2 - pH 7.4 in about 00:30:00 - 00:45:00 (indicated by the red color, not too purple, not too orange), anesthetization and dissection of pups can begin.
DISSECTION Anesthetize pups with an intraperitoneal injection of ketamine (0.01 ml and 0.05 ml of 100 mg/ml ketamine for mouse and rat pups, respectively).
When pups begin to show sedation, put them into ice till hypothermic.
Rinse aluminum foil square, scissors and head with 70% EtOH.

Decapitate pup, allowing head to fall onto aluminum foil square and move to hood. Some types of mouse tails (for ex, VMATs) need to be saved for genotyping. Prepare a numbered 1.5ml tube for each mouse. After the mouse has been decapitated, remove the tail using a fresh, sterile razor blade and place tail in numbered tube. Put all tubes on ice during the dissection. After dissection, store tubes in -80°C freezer. Alternatively, number the pups with the marker on their back, cut the tails and genotype the pups before the cultures, and no later than 1- day old. This way the pups with the same genotype can be cultured together rather than individually.

Gently remove brain into ice cold PBS-filled Petri dish as described for glia. With anterior of brain facing left on the SYLGARD® circle (dissection will be described for right handed people) use a fine forceps in the left hand to pin the two hemispheres of the brain to the SYLGARD®. Remove any remaining meninges.
Place the brain, dorsal side down, anterior facing left onto the SYLGARD®. Using forceps, pin the brain to the SYLGARD® to immobilize.
Set the magnetic stirrer on low. Chunks should swirl around in the vial gently.

Digest for approx. 00:07:00 - 00:20:00 or until chunks have fallen apart and look something like cotton candy. Periodically examine the tubes to see if segments are sticking to the stir bars; if so, tap vials to loosen tissue.
DISH AND MEDIA PREPARATION On day before dissection, take glia dishes and wash them twice with 2 mL, cold MEM :

After dissection, place tubes containing SF1C for rinsing and triturating in the incubator until ready to triturate so that the pH stays between pH 7.2 and pH 7.4 , and the media is warm. The media must be at the correct pH (indicated by the red color) or the neurons may die.
TRITURATION From this point forward, everything should be done in a sterile laminar hood with sterile technique.

Remove chunks from enzyme vial using transfer pipette and place in 15 ml conical test tube. IMPORTANT: Take great care to prevent bubble formation because bubbles are harmful to neurons. If bubbles occur, aspirate them off.

Rinse chunks 2-3 times using about 2 mL, SF1C warmed to 37 °C . After adding SF1C for each wash, flick the tube so that the cells swirl around and shake off the papain. Allow tissue to settle each time and discard as much of the supernatant as possible without exposing cells to air.
Triturate in 2 mL, room temperature SF1C with large-bore tech-tips a couple of times (when done properly the medium becomes slightly opalescent with dissociated cells).
Repeat the trituration process up to three times with a medium-, then a small-bore tech-tip. The chunks should now be completely dissociated into individual cells.
Centrifuge cells at 1000 rpm, °C, 5 minutes .
ATTACHMENT After 01:30:00 , cells should have attached to the surface of the well. Remove rings from each well using forceps, which have sterile pipette tips at the end.
Add 100 µL, GDNF (200 ng/ml) to each culture for a final concentration of 10 ng/ml GDNF (see media and solutions section how to prepare GDNF).
The next day inhibit the growth of non-neural cells with 5-fluorodeoxyuridine (FDU). Dilute 1000X stock 1:10 by adding 200 µL, stock to 1.8 mL, MEM .

Media should be changed only when necessary (change of color) – aspirate half the media and add fresh preconditioned (pre-warmed at 37 °C ). Try to avoid this step if possible as neuronal cells are very sensitive to the media exchange.
Disturb cultures as little as possible, avoiding multiple observations (especially in the first 2 days after culturing).

Process cells as needed for the individual experiment (ideally, between 5 days to 2 weeks old). If necessary after fixation/immunostaining, the coverslips can be taken off the Petri dish (cut between the glass and Sylgard with a scalpel – careful not to break the glass!!!) and mounted on a glass slide. Alternatively, cells can be cultured on free-floating coverslips, which can be later removed from dishes for the experiments.
Prepare astrocyte cultures in tissue culture flasks (225 cm 2 for rat and 75 cm 2 for mice cultures) Obtain one or two rat or five mouse pups P1-P3.
Anesthetize pups with an intraperitoneal injection of ketamine.
When pups begin to show sedation, put them On ice till hypothermic.
Rinse aluminum foil square, scissors and head with 70% EtOH.
Decapitate pup, allowing head to fall onto aluminum foil square and move to hood.
Remove the olfactory lobes and brain stem (with v-cut).

DIGESTION Place chunks in papain as with neuronal prep, without kynurenate (1 rat cortex/vial, or 5 mouse cortexes/vial). Chunks will be digested enough to triturate during the initial 00:07:00 to 00:10:00 . We judge by the appearance of the chunks (like cotton candy) when they are ready
TRITURATION AND PLATING Warm up sterile M10C-G to 37 °C for rinsing and trituration.
Remove chunks from enzyme vial using transfer pipette and place in 15 ml conical test tube
Rinse chunks 3 times using 2 mL, warm M10C-G , allowing them to settle each time and discard supernatant.
Using 1 mL, warm M10CG triturate 5-10 times using just the regular pipette tip.
Repeat trituration using the tech-tip with smaller size hole, until the chunks have all fallen apart.
Pellet the glial cells at 1000 rpm, °C, 5 minutes .

Transfer cell suspension to tissue culture flask. Use big T-225 flask for rat culture and T-75 flask for mouse culture. The yield from two rat brains is ideal for one T-225 flask. And the yield from 5 mouse brains is ideal for one T-75 flask.
Allow cells to settle for at least 02:00:00 , better leave them Overnight in incubator.
RINSING AND FEEDING For astrocyte monolayer remove M10CG from flask and squirt ~ 20 mL, cold MEM onto the base of upright tissue culture flask.
Aspirate off MEM and feed with cold M10C-G (30 ml per T-225 flask, or 15ml per T-75 flask), and leave ~2 weeks in the incubator. DO NOT ADD FDU TO FLASKS!!!!

MICROGLIA ISOLATION Once the cell layers are stable and you start seeing rounded microglial cells on top of the layers (usually 10-14 d after the cortical culture is plated) and floating in the medium. You should harvest the floating microglia at this time.
Shake the flask with a back-and-forth motion several times to help lift the microglia off.
Collect the fluid in two 15 ml conical tube and centrifuge at 3000 rpm, °C, 5 minutes . Note that the speed is higher than for regular cells centrifugation, which is normally 1,000rpm.
Remove supernate and GENTLY resuspend the cell pellet in 1 mL, warm conditioned M10CG with regular 1ml pipette tip.
Count cells on hematocytometer to determine appropriate volume for cells density @ 60,000 cells/well on neuronal cultures or 150, 000 cells/well 96 well plates for biochemical assays.

When plating on neuronal cultures place plastic ring around the well and plate cells in the ring. Remove ring after 02:00:00 . PLEASE BE SURE TO REMOVE NEURONAL MEDIA (SFIC) AND REPLACE WITH 2-3 DAY ASTROGLIAL CONDITIONED MEDIA BEFORE PLATING MICROGLIA.

Make the initial cut through the entire brain caudal to the midbrain flexure (right line, Fig 1 in Guidelines section), and the second cut rostral to the flexure, including the caudal aspect of the hypothalamus.
Remove any remaining cortex.
Lay the resulting slice flat on the SYLGARD® with the ventral side facing right, and the dorsal side to the left and the caudal aspect up.
Place the forceps through the aqueduct and push into the SYLGARD® to immobilize the slice.
Cut the ventral edge of the slice along the top of the hypothalamus as indicated by the line in Figure in the Guidelines section.

Cut into 1 mm 3 segments and place all three segments into the vial with papain solution for ventral midbrain cultures. For SN cultures only use the two outer segments and for VTA only use the inner segment. Use both SN and VTA for combined VM cultures.
Next cut approximately halfway between the new ventral edge of the slice and the aqueduct.
Replace MEM with 2 mL, SF1C .
Leave dishes with SF1C in the incubator Overnight . Alternatively, do this step on the day of the culture, using preconditioned media.
Resuspend the pellet in 0.2-0.5 ml (for mouse cultures) or 1-2 ml (for rat cultures) of media, mix and count (see below).

Add the appropriate volume to the well (into the slide ring) of the SF1C-containing dishes so that each well contains 80,000 cells (use a circular motion to spread the cells around). Again, the rings should ensure that the neurons settle into the well and do not float away or get washed away when moving the dishes.
Dilute cell suspension until 50 µL - 100 µL of media contains 80,000 cells.
Add 20 µL, diluted FDU solution to each dish (2 ml media).
Gently remove brain into ice cold PBS filled Petri dish (with one Sylgard circle – should be covered with cold PBS!).
Hemi-sect brain and then peel off meninges.
Remove any torn areas to avoid DNA release.
Shell out cortex.
Cut into 1 mm 3 chunks.
Gently allow the media to swirl around the bottom of the flask to remove unattached cells.
Shake additional ~ 00:10:00 placing the flask on a shaker at moderate speed.
Using (toothed) forceps hold head by the eyes (using left hand if you are right handed).

Gently remove brain (using a small scoop shaped scalpel) and place into Petri dish with one SYLGARD® circle stuck onto the bottom and filled with ice cold sterile PBS (use enough PBS such that the brain lying on the SYLGARD® circle is submerged)
Next, use a micro-scissor to cut around the circumference of the skull, and gently peel the skull off with forceps, being careful in case any tissue still connects it to the rest of the skull.
Using the other hand and a curved or angled forceps, pinch the scalp just behind the eyes and pull back, tearing it off.
Rinse 1/2: rinse in Nanopure dd H 2 O for 00:05:00 .
Rinse 2/2: rinse in Nanopure dd H 2 O for 00:05:00 .
Rinse 1/2: rinse in Nanopure dd H 2 O for 00:05:00 .
Rinse 2/2: rinse in Nanopure dd H 2 O for 00:05:00 .
Rinse 2/3: rinse in tissue culture water for 00:05:00 .
Rinse 3/3: rinse in tissue culture water for 00:05:00 .
Rinse 1/3: Rinse chunks using 2 mL, M10C-G , allow them to settle and discard supernatant.
Rinse 2/3: Rinse chunks using 2 mL, M10C-G , allow them to settle and discard supernatant.
Rinse 3/3: Rinse chunks using 2 mL, M10C-G , allow them to settle and discard supernatant.
Squirt 2 mL, cold MEM into each well, moving the pipette around in a circular motion to wash each well evenly.
Aspirate off MEM.

Add an HCl-cleaned, autoclaved micro-stir bar to the vial. Fill each tube with 5 mL - 10 mL, freshly made papain solution (sterile filtered with 0.22 µm filter). Place in temperature controlled 37 °C water bath with magnetic stirrers.
Wash 1/2: wash with 2 mL, cold MEM .
Wash 2/2: wash with 2 mL, cold MEM .

Rinse 1/2: Rinse chunks 2-3 times using about 2 mL, SF1C warmed to 37 °C . After adding SF1C, flick the tube so that the cells swirl around and shake off the papain. Allow tissue to settle and discard as much of the supernatant as possible without exposing cells to air.

Rinse 2/2: Rinse chunks 2-3 times using about 2 mL, SF1C warmed to 37 °C . After adding SF1C, flick the tube so that the cells swirl around and shake off the papain. Allow tissue to settle and discard as much of the supernatant as possible without exposing cells to air.

Optional Rinse: Rinse chunks 2-3 times using about 2 mL, SF1C warmed to 37 °C . After adding SF1C, flick the tube so that the cells swirl around and shake off the papain. Allow tissue to settle and discard as much of the supernatant as possible without exposing cells to air.
Rinse 1/3: Rinse chunks using 2 mL, M10C-G , allow them to settle and discard supernatant.
Rinse 2/3: Rinse chunks using 2 mL, M10C-G , allow them to settle and discard supernatant.
Rinse 3/3: Rinse chunks using 2 mL, M10C-G , allow them to settle and discard supernatant.
Resuspend pellet in 15 mL, M10C-G .
